prompt_a_response: A bishop moves any number of unoccupied squares along a diagonal, staying on the same colour, and may capture an opponent’s piece on the first occupied square it meets; it cannot jump over any pieces.

A knight moves in an “L‑shape”: it goes two squares horizontally or vertically and then one square at a right angle (or one square horizontally/vertically followed by two squares perpendicular). It may jump over any intervening pieces.
One square in any direction (horizontally, vertically, or diagonally).

A rook moves any number of unoccupied squares horizontally or vertically (along a rank or a file). It may capture an opponent’s piece by moving onto its square, but it cannot jump over any pieces. (During castling the rook also moves two squares toward the king.)
The queen may move any number of squares in a straight line horizontally, vertically, or diagonally, provided no pieces block its path; it captures an opponent’s piece by landing on its square.
A pawn may move forward either one square or, from its starting rank, two squares—so it can advance up to **two squares** on its first move.

A pawn captures by moving one square diagonally forward (to the left or right) onto a square occupied by an opponent’s piece, thereby removing that piece from the board. (The only exception is the en‑passant capture, where a pawn may capture an opposing pawn that has just advanced two squares by moving diagonally to the square the pawn passed over.)

When a pawn reaches the opponent’s farthest rank (the 8th rank for White, the 1st rank for Black), it **must be promoted**: the player replaces the pawn with a queen, rook, bishop, or knight of the same colour (their choice). The new piece must be placed on the square and the clock started before the move is considered complete.
In check.

A player can meet a check by:

1. **Moving the king** to a square where it is no longer in check.  
2. **Capturing the checking piece** with any of his pieces (including the king).  
3. **Blocking the line of attack** by interposing a piece between the checking piece and the king (possible only against a sliding piece – bishop, rook, or queen).

**Checkmate** is a position in which a player’s king is in check and that player has no legal move that can remove the check; consequently the game ends immediately with a win for the player delivering the mate.
No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess, any move that leaves or places your own king in check is illegal.
No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess there is no obligation to announce “check”; a player may do so, but it is not required.
Stalemate is a draw that occurs when the player whose turn it is to move is **not in check** and **has no legal move** available. (FIDE Laws of Chess, Art. 5.2.2)

Yes.  Under Article 9.1.2 of the FIDE Laws of Chess the players may agree to a draw at any time.  The game is considered drawn the instant the opponent accepts the offer (or, in practice, when the arbiter records the accepted offer).  The draw does **not** take effect merely when the offer is made; it takes effect only upon acceptance.

Yes. Under Article 5.2 of the FIDE Laws of Chess, the game is immediately drawn when a position is reached in which, by any possible series of legal moves, neither side can ever checkmate the opponent’s king. This is the “draw by insufficient material” rule.
50 consecutive moves by each player.
No. The game is not automatically drawn; a player (or the arbiter in certain cases) must claim a draw when the same position has occurred three times.
9 points.
5 points.
3 points.
1 point.
White moves first.
White (a light‑colored) square.
Both queens start on the **d‑file** – White’s queen on d1 and Black’s queen on d8.
Each side starts with 16 pieces: 8 pawns and 8 pieces (king, queen, 2 rooks, 2 bishops, and 2 knights).
1 point.
Each player receives ½ point for a drawn game.
When a player declares resignation, the game ends immediately and that player loses; the opponent is declared the winner (recorded as 1‑0 if White resigns, 0‑1 if Black resigns).
Extending a hand—i.e., offering a handshake to the opponent—is the customary physical signal of resignation.
No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess any move that leaves one’s own king in check is illegal; a player may not make a move elsewhere on the board that results in his king being in check.
No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess each square may contain at most one piece, so two pieces of the same colour cannot occupy the same square.
The opponent’s piece is captured: it is removed from the board (placed on the capturer’s side) and the moving piece occupies the square.
Yes. A draw by agreement is only valid when both players mutually consent to it.
The white king starts on e1 and the black king starts on e8.
No. A king may never move to a square where it would give check to the opposing king; kings cannot be placed on adjacent squares, so a king cannot deliver check.
No. A pawn may only move forward (one square, or two squares from its starting rank) and capture diagonally forward; it cannot move backward toward its own side.
Draw. A king and bishop cannot force checkmate against a lone king; it is insufficient mating material.
Draw. A king and knight cannot force checkmate against a lone king.
Yes. With correct technique, a king and queen can always force checkmate against a lone king.

A pawn may capture en passant only when **all** of the following are true:

1. **Opponent’s pawn makes a double‑step move** – it moves two squares forward from its **starting rank** (White: from rank 2 to rank 4; Black: from rank 7 to rank 5).

2. **The double‑step lands the opponent’s pawn on a square immediately adjacent** (horizontally) to the player’s pawn.

3. **The player’s pawn is on its fifth rank** (White on rank 5, Black on rank 4) – i.e., the rank from which it could capture the opponent’s pawn had that pawn moved only one square.

4
Yes. The black pawn on b4 may capture en passant, moving to **a3** (and the white pawn on a4 is removed).

No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess the en‑passant capture must be played on the very next move after the opponent’s pawn makes the two‑square advance. If the player makes any other move, the right to capture en passant is lost and cannot be exercised later.

Yes. After Black’s two‑square move c7‑c5, the white pawn on d5 may capture en passant by playing **dxc6**, removing the pawn that just passed through c6. The king on e5 and the distant rook do not affect the legality of the en‑passant capture.
No. Castling is illegal if the king is in check (or would move through or land on a square under attack).
No. Castling is illegal if the king must pass over a square that is under attack.
No. Castling is illegal if the king’s destination square (or any square it passes over) is under attack.

No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess, castling is illegal only if the king is in check, or if any square the **king** moves through (e‑g, e1‑d1‑c1 for White queenside) or the square on which it lands is under attack. Squares that only the rook traverses (such as b1 in queenside castling) may be attacked without affecting the legality of the castling move.

No. A past check does **not** affect castling rights.  
Under the FIDE Laws of Chess (Art. 3.8.2), castling is prohibited only if:

* the king has previously moved, or  
* the rook involved has previously moved, or  
* the king is currently in check, or  
* any square the king must cross (e1, f1, g1 for kingside) is under attack.

Since White’s king has never moved, the h1‑rook is still on its original square, the king is not presently in check, and f1 and g1 are empty and unattacked, White may still castle kingside. The earlier check that was already dealt with does not forfeit the right to castle.
Yes. All squares between the king and the rook must be unoccupied for a castling move to be legal.
They must move that piece (i.e., make a legal move with it) if any legal move exists; only if the piece has no legal move may they play any other legal move.
They must capture that opponent’s piece (if a legal capture is available). If no capture is possible, they may make any other legal move.
Say “j’adoube” (or “I adjust”) before touching the piece, then adjust it. This announcement removes the touch‑move obligation.

Under the FIDE Laws (Article 4.3.2), a player who touches a piece must move it **only if it can be moved to a legal square**.  
If the touched piece has **no legal moves** (e.g., it is pinned and any move would leave the king in check), the player is **not obliged to move that piece** and may make any other legal move with a different piece. If no legal move exists at all, the game ends (checkmate or stalemate).

A move is completed – and therefore cannot be taken back – the instant the player **releases the piece on its new square and then presses his clock (starting the opponent’s time)**. Once the clock has been pressed, the move is considered made.
The same hand that was used to move the pieces must be used to press the clock.

It is the moving player’s responsibility – after completing a move the player must press the clock. The opponent may point out the error, but the onus is on the player who moved to stop his/her own time.

Yes. Under the FIDE Laws a move is completed as soon as the player releases the piece on a legal square and then presses the clock (or the opponent makes a move). The piece does not have to be standing upright; it only has to be placed on the destination square. The arbiter may ask the player to stand the piece up, but that does not affect the legality or completion of the move. Therefore the opponent’s claim is invalid – the move is considered completed.

The player whose flag falls loses the game (the opponent wins), because the opponent has sufficient mating material. If the opponent lacked mating material the result would be a draw, but with mating material the flag‑fall is a loss.
Draw – the player whose flag fell does not lose because the opponent, having only a lone king, has insufficient material to force checkmate.

Yes. In any standard‑time‑control game each player must keep a scoresheet and record **both** his own move and his opponent’s move in algebraic notation. The move is written **immediately after it is made** (i.e., before pressing the clock), using the standard symbols (move number, “x” for captures, “+” for check, “#” for mate, “=Q” etc. for promotions, “O‑O”/“O‑O‑O” for castling) and the final result is entered at the end of the game.

Yes. Under Article 8.1.2 of the FIDE Laws of Chess, a player who has less than five minutes remaining on the clock is not required to record his moves, provided the time control does **not** include an increment of 30 seconds or more. In that situation he may stop keeping score.
The scoresheets are the property of the arbiter (i.e., the tournament’s arbiter).

The scoresheet is the only reliable record of what has actually happened on the board. Under the FIDE Laws (Art. 8.7), a claim of a draw by the 50‑move rule or by threefold repetition must be supported by the player’s written record so that the arbiter can verify:

* that at least 50 consecutive moves have been played without a pawn move or capture, and  
* that the same position (with the same rights to move, castle, en‑passant, etc.) has occurred three times.

If the scoresheet is incomplete, the arbiter has no way to confirm those conditions, and the claim cannot be accepted. Hence the scoresheet matters because it provides the evidence required to substantiate the draw claim.

**Procedure:**  
When the position is about to appear for the third time, the player must claim the draw **before** actually making the move that creates the repetition.

1. **Write the intended move on the scoresheet.**  
2. **Stop his own clock** (or press the clock after writing the move, but before the move is executed).  
3. **Notify the arbiter** (or make a verbal claim) that he is claiming a draw by three‑fold repetition.  

The arbiter then checks the scoresheets/board. If the claim is valid, the game is declared drawn; if not, play continues and the player must make the move he wrote.

The position must be identical in every respect — the same side to move, the same castling rights for both players, and the same en‑passant capture rights (i.e., the same pawn that could be taken en passant).

If the claim is examined and found not to meet the three‑fold‑repetition condition, the claim is rejected, the game continues from the position as it stood, and the opponent is awarded two extra minutes on his/her clock.

No. The threefold‑repetition claim does **not** require the positions to occur on consecutive moves; the same position (with the same player to move and the same rights to castle or capture en passant) may appear at any three separate times during the game.

**Procedure:**  
When the opponent’s move makes the count reach 50 moves without a pawn move or capture, the player whose turn it is must claim the draw **immediately, before making his own move or pressing his clock**. He stops his clock, calls the arbiter, and states that he is invoking the 50‑move rule. The arbiter checks the scoresheets; if the claim is correct the game is declared a draw. If the claim is made after the player has made a move, the claim is invalid.
Yes. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess, the 50‑move‑rule counter is reset whenever a pawn is moved **or** any piece is captured. Any pawn move or any capture restarts the count.
The 50‑move rule is **not automatic**; a player must claim the draw (or the arbiter must be asked to apply it). Only the 75‑move rule is an automatic draw.

No. Under the FIDE Laws (art. 6.9), a player who runs out of time loses **unless** the opponent has no possible way to deliver checkmate by any legal sequence of moves.  
With king + two knights versus a lone king, checkmate is possible (e.g., if Black’s king walks into a corner and White’s knights cooperate), so the material is not “insufficient mating material.” Consequently Black’s flag‑fall is a loss for Black and a win for White.

No. Under the FIDE Laws (Art. 6.9), a player who runs out of time loses **unless** the opponent cannot possibly checkmate by any legal sequence of moves. With king + bishop White **can** deliver a checkmate against a king + knight (the knight can be trapped or block its own king’s escape squares), so White has sufficient mating material. Therefore Black’s flag‑fall is a loss for Black, not a draw.
Yes. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess a position with K + B versus K + B where both bishops are on the same colour is a dead‑position, and the game is immediately drawn.
Yes. A king and rook can force checkmate against a lone king.

According to the FIDE Laws of Chess (Art. 7.5.1), if during a standard game it is discovered that the pieces were set up incorrectly at the start, the game is **cancelled** and a **new game is played** with the correct initial position, with the clocks reset. The aborted game has no result.

The arbiter stops the clocks, re‑orients the board so that the right‑hand corner square for each player is light, then restarts the clocks. The game continues from the same position; no penalties are applied.

If it is discovered after play has begun that the players were using the wrong colors, the game is **not replayed**. The game continues, but the result is recorded as a **loss for the player who should have had the opposite color** (i.e., the player who was playing the wrong color is deemed to have lost).

No. The move was legal. Under the FIDE Laws the *castling move* is a single move in which the king and rook end up on their proper squares; the order in which the player physically lifts and replaces the pieces is irrelevant as long as both pieces are moved before the clock is pressed. Therefore no illegal move occurred and no penalty or forced king move is required.
Two. After a player makes a second completed illegal move in a standard game, they lose the game.

The move is illegal. Under the Laws of Chess a pawn is not considered promoted until the pawn is removed and a piece of the player’s choice (queen, rook, bishop or knight) is placed on the eighth‑rank square **before** the clock is pressed.  

If a player presses the clock while the pawn is still on the promotion square:

1. The opponent may claim an illegal move (or the arbiter may intervene if he sees it).  
2. The arbiter orders the player to complete the promotion by removing the pawn and putting the chosen piece on that square.  
3. The illegal‑move penalty is applied: for the first illegal move the opponent receives a two‑minute time bonus (or the arbiter may apply the appropriate penalty under the competition rules); a second illegal move by the same player results in loss of the game.  

Thus the position is corrected to a proper promotion and the

No. Under the FIDE Laws a game ends only by checkmate, stalemate, the 50‑move rule, insufficient material, a draw agreement, a time‑control result, or a player’s explicit resignation.  Accidentally toppling your own king is treated like any other displaced piece (Article 4.2): the player must replace it on its original square and play continues.  Since the player gave no verbal or written indication of resignation, the opponent’s claim is invalid and the arbiter will order the game to continue.

No. Under the FIDE Laws (Article 11.3.1) a player is forbidden to use any electronic device—including a mobile phone—while playing a FIDE‑rated standard game, unless the arbiter expressly permits it (e.g., for a medical emergency).

No. Under the FIDE Laws of Chess, an en‑passant capture is permitted **only** when an opponent’s **pawn** makes the two‑square advance from its starting rank and passes through a square that is attacked by your pawn. The capture is made as if the pawn had moved only one square. Therefore you cannot capture any piece other than a pawn, and you cannot capture a pawn that has moved only a single square.

No. In blitz (or rapid) when there is no arbiter, the opponent may only claim a win for an illegal move **before** he makes his own reply. Once he has completed his next move, the illegal move is deemed accepted and the game continues; the claim is no longer valid.

No. Under the FIDE Laws a draw offer is only valid when it is made **after you have completed your own move on the board and before you stop your clock** (or, equivalently, before the opponent makes a move). Offering a draw while it is the opponent’s turn – i.e., before you have just moved – does not meet the rule and is not a proper draw offer.
Yes. A player may write the move on the scoresheet before completing it on the board, provided the move is legal.

The player must now make a **legal move with the same piece that made the illegal move, if that piece can make any legal move**; if that piece has no legal move, the player may make any other legal move.

The game is declared a **draw**.  
According to the FIDE Laws of Chess (Article 6.10), when both flags have fallen and it cannot be determined which fell first, the result is a draw, regardless of the position on the board.

No. A king may not move onto a square that is attacked. If an enemy piece is defended, the square it occupies is under attack by that defender, so the king would be moving into check. Therefore a king cannot legally capture a defended piece.

No. Once a rook has left its original corner square, it is considered to have moved, and even if it later returns to that square the rook may no longer be used for castling. The king and the rook involved must both be unmoved for castling to be legal.
Draw. If both flags are seen to have fallen and no claim is made, the game is declared a draw.
No. Once the game is finished, the scoresheets are signed and the result is recorded, the result is final. An illegal move discovered after that point does not alter the outcome; the arb

No. An en‑passant capture must be made on the very next move after the opponent’s pawn makes the two‑square advance. Once the player has played any other move (including castling), the right to capture en passant disappears, so attempting it two moves later is illegal.
The game is a draw – it is a stalemate. The opponent’s king is not in check and has no legal move, so the position is a stalemate regardless of material.
The player whose flag fell loses on time; the opponent wins (by time). The checkmate does not count because the flag fell before the mating move was completed.

Draw. Under the FIDE Laws a player who runs out of time loses **unless** the opponent does not have sufficient material to force (or even to deliver) a checkmate by any legal sequence. A king + knight cannot ever checkmate a lone king, even with the

The arbiter must **re‑establish the position that existed immediately before the illegal move**, give the opponent **two minutes extra time** (the penalty for an illegal move in a standard game), and then have the player who made the illegal move make a legal move with the same piece if possible (or any legal move if not). Play then continues from that corrected position. (If the illegal move were discovered only after the game had ended, the result would stand, but while the game is still in progress it is corrected as above.)

Yes. Under the FIDE Laws a pawn promotion is not final until the new piece has been **released** on the promotion square (and the pawn removed). If the player has only placed a queen there but has not let it go, they may replace it with any legal promotion piece—queen, rook, bishop or knight—before the move is completed. Once the piece is released, the choice is irrevocable.
